title: Court Reporting and Stenotype Services

narrative.executive_view: Court reporting is a pressured but not disappearing paid service. The exact NAICS scope is verbatim reporting and stenotype recording of live legal proceedings, transcription of recorded material, and real-time live captioning—not general document transcription or prerecorded captioning [S1]. AI and digital capture can compress draft transcription, scoping, proofreading, scheduling and production coordination. Yet the purchased output remains an accurate, certified record with chain-of-custody and jurisdiction-specific officer requirements. The investment case is therefore cost compression inside a durable legal workflow, offset by digital entrants, reporter disintermediation and price pass-through.
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: The production stack is splitting into capture, machine draft, human review and certification. AAERT-sponsored research describes digital capture and ASR-assisted transcription as tools that reduce cost and turnaround time, and reports that accuracy is the primary selection criterion for 96% of surveyed users [S3]. Digital reporters are trained and certified as accountable professionals rather than being unattended recorders [S4]. AI is strongest in rough drafts, speaker-aided cleanup, scheduling, exhibit handling, QA triage and routine delivery; it is weaker where realtime accuracy, oath administration, contested readback, confidentiality and final certification matter.
narrative.value_capture: Per-page pricing creates a real window for retained savings because automation can lower production cost without immediately changing the billed page. Federal examples price ordinary originals at $4.40 per page and daily originals at $7.30, with distinct copy rates [S5]. The private deposition market also charges for attendance and turnaround, but the exact mix varies by market. Capture is not assured: digital-first competitors can bid lower, law-firm procurement can demand shorter turnaround at the same price, and freelance reporters may retain key client relationships. The operator's moat rests on scheduling reliability, certified coverage, multi-jurisdiction compliance, security and preferred-vendor relationships—not on transcription labor alone.
narrative.adoption_timing: Digital reporting is already an accepted operating model in parts of the system. Florida's court standards direct digital reporting alone for several proceeding types while reserving stenography for capital and certain circuit-criminal work [S8]. Texas still requires certification to engage in shorthand reporting [S6], and California requires a deposition officer to certify the transcript or recording [S7]. I estimate 30% effective adoption of digital/AI-enabled production today and four years to 50%; the range is wide because state rules, realtime requirements, buyer acceptance and the difference between digital capture and autonomous AI all slow a uniform national transition.
narrative.consolidation_economics: This is a visibly consolidating but tiny target universe. Veritext publicly lists numerous regional court-reporting acquisitions, including a dense 2024 cadence [S9]. A sector M&A adviser lists 2025 acquisitions by Veritext, Magna, Lexitas and Circle City and describes roughly a dozen additional small unannounced deals [S11]; Magna's PE owner separately describes it as an acquisition platform [S12]. Sector valuation guidance updated with 2025 market data places firms over $1 million EBITDA at roughly 4-6x [S10]. I use 4.5x entry and 5.5x exit, but no disclosed recent court-reporting deal supplied a verifiable transaction multiple, so both are bounded estimates rather than observed comps.
narrative.terminal_demand: Paid demand survives, but the human product becomes thinner. Texas requires certified shorthand reporters [S6]; California requires an accountable deposition officer to certify the record [S7]; Florida demonstrates that digital reporting can satisfy the system for many proceedings while still imposing professional standards and retaining stenography for high-stakes matters [S8]. These mechanisms preserve paid capture, monitoring, certification and custody, not necessarily stenographic keystrokes or hours of manual transcription. Agencies that own reliable scheduling and compliance can remain valuable; agencies whose economics depend on marking up freelance pages without workflow differentiation face pressure.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The deterministic role mix is an especially severe mismatch: it is 4-digit 5614, is dominated by contact-center occupations, and omits court reporters entirely. NCRA data also show 46% of reporter members are freelancers [S2], so W-2 labor share omits much of the production cost that AI would first affect. The AAERT report is sponsor-aligned toward digital reporting [S3], while NCRA represents stenographic professionals [S2]; their directional overlap on aging supply is more reliable than either side's product claims. Exact digital share, agency-owner age and transaction multiples are not publicly reported. Captioning/CART and deposition reporting also have different demand and adoption paths.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS definition: 561492 Court Reporting and Stenotype Services
sources.what_it_supports: Defines the 6-digit scope as verbatim reporting/stenotype recording of live legal proceedings, transcription of subsequent recorded materials, and real-time live captioning, with relevant exclusions.

sources.title: NCRA Statistics
sources.what_it_supports: Current association statistics as of March 18, 2026: average reporter age 56, 46% of reporter members freelance, and only 19 approved programs.

sources.title: Court Reporting Industry 2025 Trends Report
sources.what_it_supports: AAERT-sponsored Q4 2024 survey and industry synthesis on stenographer decline, digital reporting acceptance, accuracy priorities, faster transcript delivery and ASR-enabled workflow efficiency.

sources.title: About AAERT Certifications
sources.what_it_supports: Documents professional certifications for electronic reporters and transcribers and the human standards attached to digital capture.

sources.title: Transcript Pricing Explanation and Examples
sources.what_it_supports: Official federal-court examples showing per-page original and copy pricing differentiated by delivery speed.

sources.title: Texas Court Reporters Certification: Initial Certification
sources.what_it_supports: Texas Government Code section 154.101 bars shorthand reporting without state certification and recognizes authorized reporting methods.

sources.title: California Code of Civil Procedure: Oral Deposition Transcript or Recording
sources.what_it_supports: California requires the deposition officer to certify that the witness was sworn and that the transcript or recording is a true record; certified transcripts control over rough drafts.

sources.title: Florida Court Reporting Standards and Best Practices
sources.what_it_supports: Florida's court system allows or directs digital reporting for many proceeding types, reserves stenography for specified high-stakes matters, and requires professional and record-control standards.

sources.title: Veritext Better Together: Mergers and Acquisitions
sources.what_it_supports: Veritext's acquisition archive lists a sustained cadence of regional court-reporting acquisitions, including numerous 2024 targets.

sources.title: What's My Court Reporting Firm Worth? Simple Rules of Thumb
sources.what_it_supports: Sector-specific valuation guidance updated with 2025 market data gives a 4-6x EBITDA range for court-reporting firms over $1 million EBITDA and identifies transferability risk factors.

sources.title: Court Reporting Acquisitions in 2025
sources.what_it_supports: Lists 2025 court-reporting acquisitions by at least four named consolidators and describes continued small-deal activity and long-run consolidation.

sources.title: Odyssey Investment Partners Acquires Magna Legal Services
sources.what_it_supports: Documents PE ownership of a national litigation-support provider with court reporting and a history of multiple add-on acquisitions; transaction terms were undisclosed.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: ASR and digital workflows can automate rough transcription, scoping, proofreading, scheduling, delivery and routine coordination. Accuracy review, oath/certification, chain of custody, realtime work, client relationships and high-stakes monitoring persist. The supplied roles are treated as imperfect functional proxies and the residual as a blended court-reporting/production tail.
caveats: The supplied 4-digit 5614 role mix omits SOC 23-2011 court reporters and is dominated by contact-center occupations.
Freelance reporter and scopist payments are outside W-2 wage shares, so the scored labor channel understates the true production-cost exposure.
Digital reporting adoption is not the same as unattended AI replacement; certification and human review remain.

role_judgments.rationale: Used as a scheduling, calendar and production-coordination proxy; routine booking, status and delivery tasks automate, while exception management persists.
A likely sibling-code artifact; analogous scripted outreach automates, but it is not assumed to represent core court reporting.
Billing follow-up and collections can automate partly; disputes and law-firm relationship handling remain.
Production QA, assignment and reporting automate, but reporter coverage and escalation ownership remain.
Proposal preparation automates; preferred-vendor and litigation-support selling remain relationship-driven.
Internal reporting and planning automate, while compliance, client and contractor accountability persist.
Likely a proxy artifact; analogous routine counter, intake and delivery tasks are substantially automatable.
This is the closest listed transcription proxy; ASR drafts and automated cleanup reduce manual work, with legal QA retained.
Exhibit and document routing increasingly digitize, though physical evidence and exceptions remain.
Routine user support automates; secure recording, integrations and live proceeding support remain important.
Treat the material residual as a blend of reporters, scopists, captioners and unlisted production staff: draft production automates, but live capture, realtime, QA and certification persist.

pi_dist.rationale: Per-page and turnaround pricing can initially preserve revenue while ASR lowers production cost, but faster digital alternatives and procurement pressure pass a meaningful share to clients.
caveats: Federal court schedules are a primary pricing anchor but do not determine private deposition rates.
AAERT is aligned with digital-reporting adoption and may overstate acceptance.

pi_moat.rationale: Certification, accountable record custody, coverage reliability and preferred-vendor relationships create a real but uneven moat; digital certification and multi-jurisdiction consolidators prevent it from being exclusive to stenographic incumbents.
caveats: State mechanisms differ materially, and some preserve an accountable human without preserving the incumbent agency.

t50_years.rationale: Digital reporting is accepted and ASR-assisted production is available, but fragmented state rules, high accuracy requirements and the distinction between digital capture and autonomous AI slow nationwide effective replacement.
caveats: No national series measures the share of 561492 work completed with digital capture or AI-assisted transcription.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Digital reporters and ASR-assisted workflows are established but not uniformly accepted; Florida's mixed service model and the AAERT evidence justify meaningful minority adoption rather than a majority.
caveats: This blends digital capture, ASR-assisted transcription and workflow automation; none alone equals full labor replacement.
Sponsor-aligned evidence lacks a measured national market share.

historical_analogs.value: Computer-aided transcription and courtroom digital recording automated production steps while retaining accountable reporters and certified records; current ASR extends the same task compression into draft generation and review.
historical_analogs.rationale: Florida's longstanding digital service model and current ASR workflow evidence support augmentation followed by method substitution, not instantaneous elimination of the certified record.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Gradual jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction substitution over several years
caveats: The analog does not measure private-deposition adoption or future AI accuracy.

selection.rationale: The current NCRA average reporter age is 56 and industry research describes retirement pressure. Agency owners are plausibly older than the workforce average, yielding a 40% estimate.
caveats: Reporter/member age is not agency-owner age.
NCRA membership is not a census of 561492 business owners.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: A stenographer labor shortage and retirement pipeline are documented, but no evidence specifically established a shortage of successors for agency ownership; the scored ownership condition is therefore false.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: false; true only with owner-transfer evidence
caveats: Do not reuse the labor shortage as an ownership-succession fact.

active_consolidators.rationale: The 2025 sector list supports at least four named active buyers—Veritext, Magna, Lexitas and Circle City—while Veritext and Magna separately demonstrate continuing platform behavior.
caveats: Some buyers are diversified litigation-support platforms rather than pure 561492 operators.

buy_mult.rationale: Select the lower half of the sector adviser's 4-6x range for firms over $1 million EBITDA because of freelancer dependence, client/reporter transferability and technology pressure.
caveats: The source provides advisory rules of thumb, not disclosed transaction-level comps.
The $1-10M EBITDA band is wider than the source's single over-$1M category.

exit_mult.rationale: A diversified, scaled platform could exit near the upper half of the observed 4-6x sector range because active consolidators value coverage, technology and cross-selling.
caveats: Recent named acquisitions generally do not disclose EBITDA or price.
Multiple expansion requires genuine scale and transferability, not merely AI cost savings.

pricing_structure.value: Certified transcripts are primarily billed per page with premiums for faster delivery and separate copy economics; private depositions may also carry attendance and ancillary service fees.
pricing_structure.rationale: Official federal examples directly document original and copy page rates across delivery schedules; the private-market attendance component is a disclosed industry generalization.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Per-page remains the principal transcript unit; appearance, realtime, video and exhibit fees vary by market
caveats: Federal maximum rates do not set private deposition pricing.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: Moderate technical wage opportunity and a four-year t50 are consistent: automation is already useful in production, but regulated accountability and fragmented adoption slow effective replacement.

terminal_value.justification: The paid need for an accurate, certified record survives, but stenographic production and manual transcription shrink toward capture, QA and certification. Digital methods can serve many proceedings without eliminating accountable humans.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No. The role mix is an unrepresentative 4-digit proxy; owner age uses reporter/member demographics; adoption lacks a national share; and multiples rely on sector advisory ranges rather than disclosed deal terms.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Per-page pricing can preserve an initial cost spread, but digital-first competition and procurement can lower page rates or bundle faster turnaround. That pass-through is reflected in pi_dist rather than terminal classification.

dataset_mismatch.description: The supplied 4-digit 5614 OEWS mix omits court reporters, overweights contact-center roles and excludes substantial 1099 reporter/scopist production cost from the W-2 labor share.
sensitivity: If true production cost including freelancers were used, the economic savings pool could be materially larger than deterministic V.
If the residual is dominated by high-skill realtime reporters and captioners, ai_replaceable_share could fall toward 0.38.
If coordination and post-production dominate W-2 wages, ai_replaceable_share could approach 0.58.

market_boundary.selection_basis: The thesis centers on certified legal records because they dominate the acquisition logic, while separately recognizing the code's live-captioning activities and excluding general transcription and prerecorded captioning under the Census definition.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves deterministic V materially, but does not capture off-payroll freelancer savings.
Can eliminate the selected expansion at the low end or roughly double M at the high end.

reviewer_note: Every registered URL was reopened on 2026-07-21. U.S. Legal Support's 2026 acquisition page was excluded after its live reopen returned 403; no claim depends on it.
